title: The picture we do not discuss
paras: The picture we do not discuss — that's where I want to begin. But first, before anything else: if you or a colleague are struggling, help is available now. There's no threshold you have to reach first — earlier is better than later. The Samaritans are free, day or night, on one one six, one two three. Shout is a twenty-four-seven crisis text line — text eight five two five eight. If you're in immediate danger, call nine nine nine. There's a fuller directory in the 'Where to turn' topic, towards the end of this piece.

Here's the argument. Mental health at work is a leadership and management issue before it is a wellbeing-perk issue. A caseload no one can carry, and a committee that abuses the officer who presents to it, are not fixed by a meditation app. What actually helps is workload control, senior colleagues who model and notice, and a structured programme of support — rather than a poster in the kitchen.

Planning is a caring profession that isn't very good at caring for the people who do it. The wider built environment it sits within is one of the worst sectors in the country for mental ill health. The suicide rate among construction workers is reported at around three point seven times the national average. And a 2025 survey by the Chartered Institute of Building found that eighty per cent of construction workers had felt stressed, sixty-eight per cent had experienced anxiety, and thirty-eight per cent had experienced depression over the previous year. That's certain. Academic work now describes a 'silent crisis' of suicide across construction. The figures for planning specifically are thinner, but the conditions are familiar — heavy caseloads, statutory deadlines that never stop, chronic under-resourcing in local authorities, and a culture that treats stoicism as competence. That one I'd flag as likely.

Put that against the national baseline, and the scale is clear. The 2017 Thriving at Work review found that around one in seven people at work have symptoms of a mental health condition; that some three hundred thousand people with long-term mental health problems lose their jobs every year; and that poor mental health costs employers between thirty-three billion and forty-two billion pounds annually. That's certain. None of this is a soft, optional concern at the edge of the profession. It's a workforce problem, a retention problem and — for public bodies already short of planners — a capacity problem. Treating wellbeing as a perk, rather than as core to keeping a service running, is itself part of why the service is short-staffed.

A word on how this guide is built. The body is straight reference, and I flag each claim as I go — certain, likely, or guessing. Where the piece takes a position, it's quarantined in a marked PlanRef view, and you'll hear me flag those too. And please be aware: this article discusses work-related distress and suicide.

title: The public-facing injury
paras: Now, the public-facing injury. Planners carry a stressor that desk-bound professions don't, and it deserves naming rather than shrugging off. Planning is where the public most often collides with the state over the things they care about most — their home, their street, their view — and the officer is the face of the decision. In a small but real proportion of cases, that turns into hostility. The R-T-P-I, in material published in March 2026, records planners being followed while driving, having vehicles vandalised, being abused on social media, and being treated inappropriately by elected members during committee. That's certain. It's part of a wider surge in abuse of people in public life — one the Local Government Association has been forced to confront through its 'Debate Not Hate' campaign, and one the R-T-P-I is now addressing through a Safety of Planners working group and a partnership with the Suzy Lamplugh Trust on personal safety. That's certain too.

Even short of outright abuse, the everyday job carries what psychologists call emotional labour — the sustained effort of staying calm, neutral and professional while absorbing other people's anger, disappointment and fear. Add to that the moral weight of having to recommend refusal, take enforcement action, or defend an unpopular allocation without controlling the outcome — and of doing it in public, by name, at a microphone. A single hostile committee, or one confrontational site visit, can sit with an officer for days. That's likely. The professional instinct is to treat all of this as simply part of the job, and to say nothing. And that is precisely the instinct that lets it accumulate unseen.

title: What Covid and home working changed
paras: So what did Covid and home working change? The pandemic rewired how planners work, and the settlement it left behind is genuinely double-edged. Hybrid and home working are, for most people, a real gain: flexibility, no commute, time returned to family and exercise, and better access to the profession for carers and disabled planners. Surveys consistently find that the overwhelming majority of people want to keep at least some remote working, and describe their own experience of it as positive. That's likely. Any honest treatment has to start there, because a nostalgic 'back to the office' reflex misreads what people actually value.

But the same shift carries a quieter cost that lands unevenly, and planners should see it plainly. Isolation is the core of it. One large study of over half a million workers attributed roughly a third of the rise in mental distress since the pandemic to the growth of remote work — with remote workers spending an extra hour a day alone, and those living alone hit hardest of all. That's likely. The boundary between work and home erodes. The commute that used to mark the end of the day is gone, the laptop on the kitchen table is always there, and 'always on' quietly becomes the norm. The informal repair mechanisms of an office disappear with it: the overheard 'you all right?', the corridor debrief after a bruising meeting, the senior colleague who notices you've gone quiet. Those things used to happen by accident. In a hybrid world, they have to be made to happen on purpose.

The group this hits hardest is the one the profession most needs to keep: early-career planners. IOSH research published in August 2025 found that nearly half of eighteen-to-twenty-four-year-olds say home or hybrid working makes workplace relationships harder; that over four in ten fear it restricts their development; that more than a quarter feel disconnected from their workplace — rising to thirty-six per cent of entry-level staff; and that only sixteen per cent of entry-level employees receive regular feedback. That's certain. A graduate planner who joined remotely may never have absorbed the tacit craft that used to pass across a desk — how a difficult objector is handled, how a committee report is really written, when to worry about a case — and may have no one who would notice they're drowning. And what people said they wanted was not table football, but structure: regular manager check-ins, mental health support, clear career paths, and in-person mentoring. That is the whole argument of this article, in a survey answer.

Now a PlanRef view — this is opinion, my own, on hybrid working. The answer to the isolation problem is not to march everyone back to their desks five days a week. That throws away a real gain, and it will cost you good people — particularly carers, and the early-career staff you're trying to keep. The answer is to do deliberately what the office used to do by accident. If connection, mentoring and noticing when someone is struggling no longer happen on their own, they have to be designed in: structured check-ins in the calendar, mentoring that is assigned rather than hoped for, and enough in-person time — not for its own sake, but for the human contact that catches problems early. The office was never magic. It was just a machine for accidental support — and that machine is now switched off, unless you rebuild it on purpose.

title: The value of senior colleagues
paras: Let me turn to the value of senior colleagues. If there is a single most effective, least expensive intervention in workplace mental health, it is a good senior colleague. Not a policy, not an app — a person, more experienced, who manages a team or mentors within it, and who does a handful of unglamorous things well. Their influence is disproportionate, because the line-manager relationship is the biggest single day-to-day determinant of how an employee experiences work — a point the Thriving at Work standards put at the centre. That's certain. What that looks like in practice sorts into three duties.

The first duty: manage the workload. It is the biggest lever there is, and it comes first because it dwarfs everything else. No quantity of resilience training, counselling or mindfulness offsets a caseload that is simply too big to carry — and pretending otherwise quietly tells staff the problem is their weakness rather than the volume. The Health and Safety Executive's Management Standards, the recognised framework for work-related stress, put 'demands' at the top of the six things an employer must actively manage — alongside control, support, relationships, role and change. That's certain. A senior colleague who does this well protects the team from unrealistic demand rather than passing it straight through. They prioritise ruthlessly, and say so. They push back upward when the work exceeds the resource. They make it acceptable to hand a case back. And they treat a permanently overloaded planner as a management failure to fix, not a performance to criticise. The determination clock and the political pressure are real, and they will not vanish — but how they land on an individual is a choice a manager makes. That's likely.

The second duty: model it, notice it, and signpost — don't try to fix it. Culture is set from the top by behaviour, not by statements. A head of service who answers emails at eleven at night, never takes lunch and never takes leave teaches the team that this is the standard, whatever the wellbeing policy says. One who takes their holiday, guards their evenings and talks openly about their own hard periods gives everyone else permission to be human. That's likely. Beyond modelling, the job is to notice. Hold regular, unhurried one-to-ones. Ask twice — 'no, how are you really?' And watch for the changes that precede a crisis: withdrawal, cynicism, uncharacteristic mistakes, a good planner going quiet. When someone does disclose, the senior colleague's role is emphatically not to be the therapist. It is to listen without diagnosing, to know the routes — the employee assistance programme, occupational health, the R-T-P-I helpline, the G-P, the Samaritans — and to walk the person to the door of the right help, while adjusting the work so recovery is possible. That's certain.

And the third duty: back your people against abuse. Where the public-facing injury is concerned, senior colleagues have a specific and non-negotiable duty — to stand behind their officers. That means public support for an officer attacked at committee or by an applicant, not a diplomatic silence. It means robust, and used, policies on abuse and lone-worker safety. And crucially, it means a debrief and a check-in after the hostile meeting or the difficult site visit, rather than moving straight on to the next case as though nothing happened. An officer who knows the organisation will back them can absorb a great deal. One who feels exposed and alone cannot — and will eventually leave. That's likely.

title: A programme of support
paras: That brings us to a programme of support. Individual good managers are necessary but not sufficient, because they come and go, and their quality varies. What turns good intentions into something dependable is a programme — a structured, named, resourced set of support that does not rely on happening to have a kind boss. The evidence and the survey responses point the same way. People want structure, not gestures.

Start with mentoring and peer support. Mentoring is the backbone, and its value in wellbeing is widely underestimated, because it is misunderstood. A mentor is not a counsellor, and should never try to be. The value is prevention, early detection and connection. A trusted person outside the direct line-management chain gives a planner somewhere to say 'I am struggling' without it feeling like a performance issue. A senior mentor who is honest about their own difficult spells dismantles the corrosive belief that everyone else is coping. And a mentor is often the first to spot the early signs, and to know where to point someone next. That's likely. The infrastructure already exists to build on — the R-T-P-I runs a mentoring scheme, and many firms and authorities run buddying for new entrants. It costs little to give it an explicit wellbeing dimension, and to make it assigned rather than optional — so the quiet junior who would never seek a mentor is given one. Peer-support networks, and structured buddying for returners and for anyone carrying contentious casework, do similar work laterally. That's likely too.

Then there is mindfulness, and the individual tools — useful, and not a substitute. Mindfulness earns a place here, with a caveat that matters. The evidence from randomised trials is that mindfulness-based programmes produce small-to-moderate reductions in stress for individuals, and app-based versions can help at the margin. For a planner who finds it useful, it is a legitimate tool. That's likely. The caveat is that mindfulness addresses the individual's response to pressure, not the pressure itself. And there is a well-aimed critique — sometimes labelled 'McMindfulness' — that employers reach for it precisely because it is cheaper and less confronting than fixing workload, and that offering meditation to a team drowning in cases can read as telling them their stress is a personal failing to breathe away. Also likely. The honest position is simple. Individual coping tools — mindfulness, exercise, sleep, boundaries, talking therapies — are worth having and worth offering. But they sit on top of structural fixes, never instead of them. A mindfulness session paired with a lighter caseload is support. The same session offered instead of one is a fig leaf.

So what does a good programme look like, assembled? A credible programme has a few recognisable parts. A clear, communicated plan that names the support available. Trained line managers who can hold a wellbeing conversation — since most are promoted for technical skill and never taught this. A confidential route to real help, through an employee assistance programme and occupational health. Mental Health First Aiders, to spot signs and signpost — useful, though the evidence is that training first-aiders raises awareness more than it changes outcomes, so they supplement good management rather than replace it. Assigned mentoring and peer support. And underneath all of it, honest monitoring of workload and wellbeing, so the organisation notices trouble before it becomes turnover. That's likely. None of this is exotic. And most of it is cheap, relative to the cost of losing and replacing experienced planners — that last point is certain.

title: The duties behind all of this
paras: A word now on the duties behind all of this. For anyone who needs the argument in the language of obligation rather than decency, the duties are real and worth knowing. Employers owe a duty of care, and the Health and Safety at Work et cetera Act 1974 extends to work-related stress, which employers are expected to assess and manage. The H-S-E Management Standards — demands, control, support, relationships, role and change — are the tool for doing so. That's certain. Under the Equality Act 2010, a mental health condition can amount to a disability, and where it does, the employer must make reasonable adjustments and must not discriminate. Also certain. The Thriving at Work standards set out what good looks like — a mental-health plan, awareness, open conversations, decent working conditions, effective people management, and routine monitoring. And the international standard I-S-O 45003, published in 2021, is the first global framework for managing psychological health and safety at work. That's certain too. Many public bodies have also signed the Mental Health at Work Commitment. The direction of all of it is the same: psychological safety is moving from a nice-to-have to a managed obligation — and the organisations ahead of that curve are the ones that will hold onto their people.

title: What it comes down to
paras: So what does it come down to? For the individual planner, a few things genuinely help, and they are worth doing without waiting for permission. Talk to someone early, rather than at crisis. Use the support that exists before you are desperate. Protect your boundaries, even when the caseload resists it. Debrief the bad days rather than swallowing them. And remember that asking for help is a professional skill, not a professional failure. That's likely. But the weight of this article deliberately sits on the organisation and the senior colleague, because that is where the leverage is. An individual can manage their response to an impossible job. Only the organisation can make the job possible.

The thread running through all of it is that wellbeing is built, not bolted on. It is in the size of the caseload. The behaviour of the person at the top. The mentor a graduate is actually given. The debrief after the hostile committee. And the structured programme that does not depend on luck. Get those right, and the app and the fruit basket are pleasant extras. Get them wrong, and no perk in the world will hold the profession together. The planners we lose to burnout and to abuse are not weak. They are, very often, the ones who cared the most — in a system that did not care enough for them. That is a choice, and it can be made differently.

One last PlanRef view — this is opinion, and it is the verdict. If you run a team, take this away: the most powerful mental-health intervention you control is not a subscription to a meditation app. It is a manageable caseload, your own visible example, and a mentor for every junior who needs one. Reach for mindfulness and resilience training first, and you are treating the symptom while leaving the cause. Worse — you are quietly telling an overloaded, abused workforce that their distress is a personal shortcoming to breathe away. Fix the workload, back your officers in public, and build support into the structure rather than pinning it to the kitchen wall — and the softer tools finally do some good on top. Wellbeing is a leadership responsibility, and delegating it to an app is the tell of an organisation that has decided not to lead. I hold that firmly, and only evidence should move it.

title: Where to turn
paras: Where to turn. If you or a colleague are struggling, these are the main routes to support. There is no threshold you must reach before using them — earlier is better than later.

In a crisis, or if you just need to talk. The Samaritans are free, day or night, on one one six, one two three — or by email, at jo at samaritans dot org. Shout is a free, twenty-four-seven crisis text line — text eight five two five eight. And if life is in immediate danger, call nine nine nine.

For planners. The R-T-P-I offers members a free mental health and wellbeing helpline and a wellbeing hub, plus free legal advice, via the R-T-P-I website — and there is the emerging Safety of Planners guidance on abuse and lone-worker safety.

Across the built environment. LionHeart supports R-I-C-S professionals and their families, with counselling and practical help. And Mates in Mind, and the Lighthouse Construction Industry Charity — which runs a twenty-four-seven helpline — support the wider construction sector.

And through work and the N-H-S. There is your employer's Employee Assistance Programme — confidential counselling, usually free — and occupational health. There are N-H-S Talking Therapies, with self-referral in England for anxiety and depression. And there is your G-P. Mind's Mental Health at Work portal, and the Hub of Hope directory, will help you find more.

title: Practice points
paras: Finally, some practice points — standalone takeaways for quick reference. Most are aimed at senior colleagues and organisations, because that is where the leverage sits.

First: treat wellbeing as capacity, not perk. A profession short of planners cannot afford to lose experienced people to burnout and abuse — and poor mental health costs employers between thirty-three and forty-two billion pounds a year nationally. That's certain. Second: workload is the biggest lever. No app or resilience course offsets an unmanageable caseload. Protect the team from unrealistic demand, prioritise openly, and push back upward — 'demands' tops the H-S-E stress standards. Certain. Third: name the public-facing injury. Abuse, emotional labour and moral weight are real occupational stressors for planners, now recognised by the R-T-P-I Safety of Planners work and the Local Government Association's 'Debate Not Hate'. Certain. Fourth: back your officers in public. Support the officer attacked at committee or on site. Have, and use, abuse and lone-worker policies. And debrief after hostile meetings, rather than moving straight to the next case. Fifth: model it from the top. Teams copy what seniors do, not what the policy says. Take your leave, guard your evenings, and talk openly about pressure. Likely. Sixth: notice, and ask twice. Regular, unhurried one-to-ones. Watch for withdrawal, cynicism and uncharacteristic slips. 'No, how are you really?' A quiet, capable planner going quiet is a signal. Seventh: signpost, do not diagnose. A manager's job is to listen, adjust the work, and know the routes — the employee assistance programme, occupational health, the R-T-P-I helpline, the G-P, the Samaritans — not to be the therapist. Certain. Eighth: do on purpose what the office did by accident. Hybrid working removed the corridor debrief and the overheard 'you okay?'. Build connection, mentoring and noticing back in deliberately — do not just order everyone back to their desks. Ninth: protect the early-career hardest. IOSH, in 2025: forty-eight per cent of eighteen-to-twenty-four-year-olds find hybrid working harms workplace relationships, thirty-six per cent of entry-level staff feel disconnected, and only sixteen per cent get regular feedback. They want check-ins and in-person mentoring, not perks. Certain. Tenth: assign mentors — do not leave it to chance. Mentoring's wellbeing value is prevention, early detection and connection, not therapy. The quiet junior who would never ask is the one who most needs one allocated. Likely. Eleventh: mindfulness on top, never instead. It gives small-to-moderate individual stress relief and is worth offering — but paired with a lighter caseload it is support; offered in place of one, it is a fig leaf. Likely. Twelfth: build a programme, not a poster. A communicated plan, trained line managers, a confidential route through the employee assistance programme and occupational health, mental health first aiders, assigned mentoring, and honest monitoring of workload and wellbeing. Thirteenth: know the duties. The duty of care and the H-S-E Standards cover work-related stress. The Equality Act 2010 requires reasonable adjustments where a mental health condition is a disability. And Thriving at Work and I-S-O 45003 set the good-practice bar. Certain. Fourteenth: ask for help early — it is a skill, not a failure. For the individual: talk before crisis, use support before you are desperate, protect boundaries, and debrief the bad days rather than swallowing them. And fifteenth: the ones we lose often cared the most. Burnout and abuse take the committed, not the weak, in systems that did not care enough back. That is a choice an organisation can make differently.

title: Sources
paras: And to close, the sources. These are the authoritative sources relied on, each with what it supports and its date. Headline statistics should be re-checked against the primary source before republication, as survey and prevalence figures are periodically restated.

First, the Chartered Institute of Building — the C-I-O-B — 2025 survey. That supports the built-environment prevalence figures: eighty per cent stressed, sixty-eight per cent anxiety, thirty-eight per cent depression over the past year. Second, Mates in Mind and Office for National Statistics analysis of the construction sector. That is the suicide risk among construction workers, reported at around three point seven times the national average — the exact multiple and date are to re-verify. Third, Thriving at Work, the Stevenson and Farmer review of mental health and employers, of the 26th of October 2017. That supports the prevalence of around one in seven at work, the three hundred thousand annual job losses, the thirty-three to forty-two billion pound employer cost, and the six core plus enhanced standards. Fourth, the R-T-P-I's 'Protecting the wellbeing and safety of planners', of the 17th of March 2026, together with the R-T-P-I mental health helpline and wellbeing hub. That covers abuse and the safety of planners, the Safety of Planners working group, the Suzy Lamplugh Trust partnership, and member support. Fifth, the Local Government Association's 'Debate Not Hate' campaign, and the House of Lords Library briefing on abuse of councillors — the wider surge in abuse of people in public life that planners sit within. Sixth, IOSH research on home and remote working and young workers, of the 28th of August 2025 — the disproportionate effect of hybrid working on early-career staff: relationships, development, disconnection, feedback and mentoring gaps. Seventh, the remote work and mental distress study from the Federal Reserve Bank of New York, the University of Virginia and Harvard — five hundred and eighty-eight thousand workers, 2011 to 2024. That supports isolation as the mechanism, roughly a third of the post-pandemic rise in distress being linked to remote work, and those living alone being worst affected. It is U-S data — treat it as indicative for the U-K. Eighth, the meta-analyses of workplace mindfulness-based interventions — randomised controlled trials, 2020 to 2024 — and the 'McMindfulness' critique. Small-to-moderate individual stress reduction, and the argument that it must not replace structural fixes. And ninth, the H-S-E Management Standards for work-related stress, I-S-O 45003 of 2021, the Equality Act 2010, and the Health and Safety at Work et cetera Act 1974. Those are the employer duties and frameworks — demands, control, support, relationships, role and change; psychological health and safety; reasonable adjustments; and the duty of care.

This was written as at the 21st of July 2026, for England and the U-K. The reference content is checked; the PlanRef view boxes are clearly-marked opinion. Statistics and organisational resources change, so re-check the figures and the support links before relying on them. And if you are struggling, the Samaritans are free on one one six, one two three — day or night.
